raga.name: Yaman
raga.description: One of the most fundamental ragas in Hindustani classical music. The raised Ma (tivra Ma) gives it a distinctive brightness compared to Bilaval. Often the first serious raga taught to students. Creates a mood of devotion and quiet joy.

forbidden.name: Shuddh Madhyam
forbidden.reason: Yaman uses only tivra (raised) Ma. Shuddh Ma belongs to Bilaval thaat and would destroy the Kalyan character.

movement.aroha: N R G M' P D N S'
movement.avaroha: S' N D P M' G R S
movement.pakad_string: N R G, R G M' P, G M' D N

characteristic_phrases.context: Resolution to Sa through lower tetrachord

note_rules.Sa_approach: Approached from Ni below (N S), rarely directly from Re descending
note_rules.Ma_treatment: Always tivra. Used as a stepping stone, not a resting note
note_rules.Ni_role: Extremely important — Yaman alaap often begins with Ni from the lower octave
note_rules.Ga_role: Vadi note — receives extended treatment and emphasis in alaap

vadi.role: King note — the emotional centre of the raga. Should receive the most musical emphasis and longest sustain in alaap.

samvadi.role: Queen note — supports the vadi. A perfect 5th above Ga, it anchors the upper register.

context.aesthetic: Evokes the calm beauty of early evening — transitioning from day to night. The raised Ma creates a sense of brightness and uplift despite the nighttime association.

western_equivalent.relationship: The Lydian mode is identical to the Kalyan thaat. The raised 4th degree (#4 / tivra Ma) is the defining characteristic of both systems.
common_lydian_songs: Flying in a Blue Dream (Joe Satriani)

lofi.notes: Lydian brightness pairs beautifully with lofi warmth. The major 7th (Ni) and #4 (tivra Ma) create dreamy, nostalgic textures. Use slow Ga-centered melodies over mellow beats.

jazz.notes: Lydian is native to jazz harmony (Lydian Chromatic Concept). Maps directly to maj7#11 chords. Works over Cmaj7#11, Dmaj7, and related voicings.

ambient.notes: The serene, devotional mood translates perfectly to ambient textures. Sustained Ga and Ni over drone-like pads create meditative soundscapes.

classical_fusion.notes: Strong melodic structure adapts well to orchestral or chamber arrangements. The clear aroha/avaroha gives composers a framework to build on.

electronic.notes: The bright Lydian tonality works with progressive and melodic electronic styles. Arpeggiations of Yaman phrases over synth pads are effective.

r_and_b.notes: The major 7th and romantic mood overlap with R&B harmonic sensibilities. The #4 adds an exotic colour to standard progressions.

rock.notes: Lydian is used in rock (Satriani, Vai) but Yaman's meditative phrasing may need adaptation to rock energy levels.

hip_hop.notes: Yaman melodic fragments work as sample material or melodic hooks. The devotional mood adds depth to introspective tracks.

observations: Both recordings confirm Kalyan thaat and Lydian mode
Ni consistently appears as the most prominent melodic note (12.4-12.6%), ahead of textbook vadi Ga (3.6-6.3%)
All 7 Kalyan thaat notes detected in the full performance
Tivra Ma clearly present (0.8-7.0%) — shuddh Ma absent or negligible
Sa dominates at 56-58% due to alaap style dwelling on tonic
